titleStmt.title: Analytical Modeling of k 33 Mode Partial Electrode Configuration for Loss Characterization

affiliation.orgName: International Center for Actuators and Transducers (ICAT)
International Center for Actuators and Transducers (ICAT)
International Center for Actuators and Transducers (ICAT)
International Center for Actuators and Transducers (ICAT)

analytic.title: Analytical Modeling of k 33 Mode Partial Electrode Configuration for Loss Characterization

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Accurate determination of three types of losses (dielectric, elastic and piezoelectric) in piezoelectric materials is critical, since they are closely related to the performance of highpower piezoelectric devices. The Standard 𝑘𝑘 33 mode has a number of serious deficits that hinder researchers from determining accurate physical parameters and losses. In order to overcome such deficits, "partial electrode" has been devised and proposed. This study provides analytical derivation process and proposes parameter determination method by utilizing analytical solutions. Compared to finite element analysis, analytical solutions show 0.1 % difference in resonance frequencies and 2 % difference in mechanical quality factors, proving themselves as valid modeling. The analytical solutions are fitted to experimental data to determine physical parameters and losses. The 𝑘𝑘 33 (electromechanical coupling factor) values were calculated with determined values from curve fitting in two different ways and show good agreement to each other.

div.head: I. Introduction
div.p: For the past decades, piezoelectric materials have been proven to be better than electromagnetic (EM) counterparts when being utilized as ultrasonic motors, sonar transducer devices and electrical transformers, since they have higher energy densities in smaller volumes and generates no electromagnetic noise. Accurate loss determination of piezoelectric material is crucial for the following two reasons: First, the bottleneck of high-power piezoelectric devices has been identified as heat generation, which severely degrades their performances [1][2]. This heat generation can be explained by three types of losses: elastic (tan 𝜙𝜙), dielectric (tan 𝛿𝛿), and piezoelectric losses (tan 𝜃𝜃). Second, finite element analysis has become imperative for devising and optimizing piezoelectric devices [3][4][5]. Exact values of intensive/extensive parameters and losses are required for the accurate simulation results of finite element analysis, especially near resonance frequencies. Therefore, it is important to understand three types of losses in different vibration modes, as well as elucidate loss mechanism.

Each dielectric, elastic, and piezoelectric loss is further classified into "intensive" and "extensive" subsets according to the constraint conditions of the vibration modes: intensive parameters, such as stress (𝑋𝑋) and electric field (𝐸𝐸), are the ones that rely upon the amount of matter or total volume of the system, whereas extensive parameters, such as strain (𝑥𝑥) and dielectric displacement (𝐷𝐷), are the ones that depend on the volume of given system. Followed by the statement of intensive and extensive parameters, the following equations are the definitions of intensive and extensive losses [6]: 𝜀𝜀 𝑋𝑋 * = 𝜀𝜀 𝑋𝑋 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝛿𝛿 ′ ) , 𝑠𝑠 𝐸𝐸 * = 𝑠𝑠 𝐸𝐸 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜙𝜙 ′ ) , 𝑑𝑑 * = 𝑑𝑑(1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜃𝜃 ′ ) ; 𝜅𝜅 𝑥𝑥 * = 𝜅𝜅 𝑥𝑥 (1 + 𝑗𝑗 tan 𝛿𝛿) , 𝑐𝑐 𝐷𝐷 * = 𝑐𝑐 𝐷𝐷 (1 + 𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜙𝜙) , ℎ * = ℎ(1 + 𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜃𝜃).
The losses can be represented as tangent angle supposing that the loss values are not large and handled primarily as viscous friction from the viewpoint of mathematical simplicity.

The first three equations explain intensive losses, which are primed; 𝜀𝜀 𝑋𝑋 , 𝑠𝑠 𝐸𝐸 and 𝑑𝑑 are relative permittivity under constant 𝑋𝑋, elastic compliance under constant 𝐸𝐸 and piezoelectric constant, respectively. Primed losses are defined as losses under constant intensive parameters.

The latter three equations explain extensive losses, which are non-primed; 𝜅𝜅 𝑥𝑥 , 𝑐𝑐 𝐷𝐷 and ℎ are inverse permittivity under constant 𝑥𝑥 , elastic stiffness under constant 𝐷𝐷 and inverse piezoelectric constant, respectively. Therefore, the non-primed losses are defined as losses under constant extensive parameters.

Up to date, Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineering (IEEE) have proposed a standard method for parameter determination with different vibration modes [7]. One of the problems with IEEE Standard is that it introduces only one type of elastic loss, which results in mechanical quality factor at resonance frequency (𝑄𝑄 𝐴𝐴 ) is equal to that at antiresonance (𝑄𝑄 𝐵𝐵 ).

However, there exist significant divergence with the experimental data (e.g. 𝑄𝑄 𝐴𝐴 < 𝑄𝑄 𝐵𝐵 in PZT [8]). This issue was resolved by explicitly introducing the "piezoelectric loss". Another issue of IEEE is a limited parameter determination [7,9]. For example, the IEEE Standard 𝑘𝑘 31 mode specimen can only provide intensive parameters [9], while the Standard 𝑘𝑘 33 or 𝑘𝑘 𝑡𝑡 specimen can exclusively provide "extensive" elastic parameters [10], causing error accumulation for obtaining intensive elastic parameters from the transformation formulas. In addition, the Standard 𝑘𝑘 33 mode also suffers from small capacitance and fringing electric field that are originated from its intrinsic structure [11][12][13].

In order to resolve such issues, partial electrode (PE) configuration method was introduced to realize the mechanical excitation measurement, in contrast to the "Standard" specimen with pure electrical excitation. PE configuration sample has two main parts: center part, which is electrically excited and behaves as a mechanical "actuator", and side part, which is pure mechanical load excited by the center part and can be modified with electrical constraints. In our previous paper [9], the 𝑘𝑘 31 mode PE was designed in order to experimentally determine both intensive and extensive elastic compliances and losses. It enables direct determination of extensive elastic parameters with non-electrode (NE) specimen, significantly reducing the error for extensive elastic parameters. For the 𝑘𝑘 33 mode, the PE design, as shown in Figure 1, are composed of the open circuit (OC) side load, which corresponds to antiresonance drive of the sample, short circuit (SC) load, which corresponds to resonance drive, and side electrode (SE), which enables direct measurement of "intensive" elastic compliance and loss. The PE configuration for 𝑘𝑘 33 mode gives great degree of benefits: it resolves small capacitance issue in the standard 𝑘𝑘 33 mode specimen because the center part gives 100 -200 higher capacitance values than the standard specimen. Also, the users can avoid specimen setup issue. When the sample is clamped in the vibration direction on the Standard 𝑘𝑘 33 specimen, a distortion of admittance spectrum can happen due to the sample vibration constraint [11,14]. Furthermore, due to the length consideration of the side part, the parameter distortion effect due to fringing electric field is smaller than the standard sample [11], and PE enable direct determination of intensive elastic properties by putting electrode on the side.

In this paper, we provide detailed derivation process for analytical solutions for 𝑘𝑘 33 mode PE configuration for providing the determination process of the intensive and extensive piezoelectric parameters and corresponding losses. The derived admittance equations are validated by the comparison with finite element analysis (FEA) and experimental admittance curves.

div.head: II. Methods
div.p: In order to solve analytical solutions and generate admittance curves for 𝑘𝑘 33 mode PE configuration, Wolfram Mathematica (version 11.1, Wolfram Research, Champaign, IL) was utilized. The FEA software package (ATILA++ distributed by Micromechatronics, Inc., State College, PA) was used to validate admittance curves from analytical solutions, which can integrate all kinds of losses, including piezoelectric loss, in the simulation. The comparison was done between admittance curves from ATILA FEA and analytical solutions with the exactly same input parameters. Also, all samples, PE OC, PE SC and PE SE, were prepared and measured with Precision Impedance Analyzer (4294A, Agilent Technology, Santa Clara, CA) with constant peak voltage of 0.1 mV to avoid possible peak distortion [15]. In order to determine the 𝑘𝑘 33 mode related parameters for the PE admittance curve fitting, the standard 𝑘𝑘 31 sample were prepared in parallel and measured with the same impedance analyzer for precisely determining the center part's physical parameters in advance. The physical parameters and losses were determined as average, and errors were determined with error propagation method. For piezoelectric material, PIC 255 (PI Ceramics GmbH, Lederhose, Germany), soft PZT with morphotropic phase boundary composition, was used and Au was sputtered to create electrodes. The bulk ceramic block was poled and cut to create plate with the dimension of 20 × 2.5 × 0.5 mm (poled along 20 mm direction). Then, the center of the plate was electrode and re-poled along 0.5 mm direction. The measured admittance curves then fitted with analytical solutions in order to determine related physical parameters.

div.head: III. Derivation of Analytical Solutions for Partial Electrode Configuration
A. General and Mechanical Wave Equations
div.p: Consider the following geometry for PE configuration, as shown in Figure 2. The effective coordinate is defined as shown. The 𝑥𝑥 , 𝑦𝑦 and 𝑧𝑧 direction are along with the configuration's length (𝑙𝑙), width (𝑤𝑤), and thickness (𝑡𝑡) direction, respectively. The numbers in the parenthesis in the axis correspond with the indices of physical parameters that are subscripted. The portion of center electrode is denoted as "a" (0 < a < 1). The zero coordinate is set at the center of the bar for convenience, since the configuration is exactly symmetrical in 𝑥𝑥 direction. It should be noted that this analysis is based on 1D assumption that 𝑙𝑙 >> 𝑤𝑤, 𝑡𝑡 and only vibration along the length is considered. Note that the slight discrepancy with the 3D ATILA simulation results will be attributed from this simplified assumption. The piezoelectric constitutive equations for 𝑘𝑘 33 mode PE are given by:
div.formula: 𝑥𝑥 1𝑐𝑐 = 𝑠𝑠 11 𝐸𝐸 𝑋𝑋 1𝑐𝑐 + 𝑑𝑑 31 𝐸𝐸 3𝑐𝑐(7)
𝐷𝐷 3𝑐𝑐 = 𝑑𝑑 31 𝑋𝑋 1𝑐𝑐 + 𝜀𝜀 0 𝜀𝜀 33 𝑋𝑋 𝐸𝐸 3𝑐𝑐(8)
𝑥𝑥 1𝑠𝑠 = 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸 𝑋𝑋 1𝑠𝑠 + 𝑑𝑑 33 𝐸𝐸 1𝑠𝑠(9)
𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 = 𝑑𝑑 33 𝑋𝑋 1𝑠𝑠 + 𝜀𝜀 0 𝜀𝜀 33 𝑋𝑋 𝐸𝐸 1𝑠𝑠(10)
div.p: For convenience, we separated constitutive equations for electrically excited center part and side load part. In above equations, the subscripts "𝑐𝑐" and "𝑠𝑠" denotes center part and side part, respectively. Note that these equations are written with respect to the effective coordinate shown in Figure 2. Now, consider the following harmonic vibration equation, which is given by:
div.formula: 𝜌𝜌 𝜕𝜕 2 𝑢𝑢(𝑥𝑥, 𝑡𝑡) 𝜕𝜕𝑡𝑡 2 = 𝜕𝜕𝑋𝑋 1 𝜕𝜕𝑥𝑥 (11) 𝑢𝑢(𝑥𝑥, 𝑡𝑡) = 𝑢𝑢(𝑥𝑥)𝑒𝑒 𝑗𝑗𝑗𝑗𝑡𝑡(12)
div.p: For the center part, by using constitutive equations and harmonic vibration equations, it is obtained:
div.formula: 𝜕𝜕 2 𝑢𝑢 1𝑐𝑐 𝜕𝜕𝑡𝑡 2 = 1 𝜌𝜌𝑠𝑠 11 𝐸𝐸 𝜕𝜕 2 𝑢𝑢 1𝑐𝑐 𝜕𝜕𝑥𝑥 2(13)
div.p: This expression is the same as "wave differential equation" that can be found elsewhere. The velocity of the wave for the above equation is define as:
div.formula: 𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 = �1 𝜌𝜌𝑠𝑠 11 𝐸𝐸 ⁄(14)
div.p: where 𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 is sound velocity related to compliance 𝑠𝑠 11 𝐸𝐸 . The general solution of the Eq. ( 14)
for 𝑢𝑢(𝑥𝑥) would be in terms of sine and cosine function, which is of the form:
div.formula: 𝑢𝑢 1𝑐𝑐 (𝑥𝑥) = 𝐴𝐴 𝑐𝑐 sin � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 𝑥𝑥� + 𝐵𝐵 𝑐𝑐 cos � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 𝑥𝑥�(15)
div.p: Since the displace function is asymmetric, but 0 at the center (center of gravity), cosine term should vanish. Therefore, for center part, we have the following equation of displacement:
div.formula: 𝑢𝑢 1𝑐𝑐 (𝑥𝑥) = 𝐴𝐴 𝑐𝑐 sin � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 𝑥𝑥�(16)
div.p: Where 𝐴𝐴 𝑐𝑐 is constant. The wave equation for the side part should be considered separately.

Due to different electrical boundary conditions, OC and SC, and SE should be separately considered. For the side part of OC and SC, we have 𝐷𝐷constant conditions. In the similar manner, it is obtained:
div.formula: 𝜕𝜕 2 𝑢𝑢 1𝑠𝑠 𝜕𝜕𝑡𝑡 2 = 1 𝜌𝜌𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 𝜕𝜕 2 𝑢𝑢 1𝑠𝑠 𝜕𝜕𝑥𝑥 2(17)
div.p: The solution to the above wave equation is given by:
div.formula: 𝑢𝑢 1𝑠𝑠 (𝑥𝑥) = 𝐴𝐴 𝑠𝑠 sin � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 33 𝐷𝐷 𝑥𝑥� + 𝐵𝐵 𝑠𝑠 cos � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 33 𝐷𝐷 𝑥𝑥�(18)
div.p: where 𝑣𝑣 33 𝐷𝐷 is sound velocity related to compliance 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 , and 𝐴𝐴 𝑠𝑠 and 𝐵𝐵 𝑠𝑠 are constants. The side part does not have center of gravity, so it does not vanish at 𝑥𝑥 = 0. For SE configuration, 𝐸𝐸constant condition should be considered. In similar manner, it is obtained:
div.formula: 𝑢𝑢 1𝑠𝑠 (𝑥𝑥) = 𝐴𝐴 𝑠𝑠 sin � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 33 𝐸𝐸 𝑥𝑥� + 𝐵𝐵 𝑠𝑠 cos � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 33 𝐸𝐸 𝑥𝑥�(19)

div.head: B. Electrical Boundary Conditions
div.p: For 𝑘𝑘 33 PE OC and SC, 𝐷𝐷 -constant condition should be considered. For OC, assuming voltage applied to the center part is small, it can be assumed that 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 is zero. For SC, the expression for 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 should be derived according to the voltage constraint. Because of the short circuit condition, the potential difference between 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ and 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ should be zero even though the electric field distributes in between:
div.formula: 𝑉𝑉 1s = -� 𝐸𝐸 1𝑠𝑠 𝑑𝑑𝑥𝑥 𝑙𝑙 2 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 = 0(20)
div.p: By using constitutive equations and Eq. ( 20), and assuming that 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 is constant along the length direction, the following relationship can be obtained:
div.formula: � 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 𝑑𝑑𝑥𝑥 𝑙𝑙 2 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 = 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 � 𝑙𝑙 2 - 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 �(21)
div.p: With Eq. ( 21), the expression for 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 can be obtained from Eq. ( 10) and ( 18), taking into account the relationship between displacement and stress, which is shown in Eq. ( 11):
div.formula: 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 = 2𝑑𝑑 33 �𝐴𝐴 𝑠𝑠 �sin � 𝑙𝑙𝜔𝜔 2𝑣𝑣 33 𝐷𝐷 � -sin � 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙𝜔𝜔 2𝑣𝑣 33 𝐷𝐷 �� + 𝐵𝐵 𝑠𝑠 �cos � 𝑙𝑙𝜔𝜔 2𝑣𝑣 33 𝐷𝐷 � -cos � 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙𝜔𝜔 2𝑣𝑣 33 𝐷𝐷 ��� 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸 𝑙𝑙(1 -𝑎𝑎)(22)
div.p: For simplicity, let us express 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 with the following equation:
div.formula: 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 = 𝑑𝑑 33 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸 𝑥𝑥 1𝑠𝑠,𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎(23)
div.p: Where, 𝑥𝑥 1𝑠𝑠,𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎 is average value of strain 𝑥𝑥 1𝑠𝑠 over the range of 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ and 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ .
For 𝑘𝑘 33 SE, 𝐸𝐸constant condition should be considered. Furthermore, due to charge neutralization originated from surface free charges that compensate surface bound charges, 𝐸𝐸 1𝑠𝑠 should be zero.

div.head: C. Mechanical Boundary Conditions
div.p: The mechanical boundary conditions are universal for all the PE configurations. First, the displacement should be continuous at 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ . By Equating Eq. ( 16) to Eq. ( 18), the following equations are obtained for the displacement continuation for OC and SC:
div.formula: 𝐴𝐴 𝑐𝑐 sin � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 � = 𝐴𝐴 𝑠𝑠 sin � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 33 𝐷𝐷 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 � + 𝐵𝐵 𝑠𝑠 cos � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 33 𝐷𝐷 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 � (24
div.p: For SE, the equation can simply be obtained by equating Eq. ( 16) and ( 19):
div.formula: 𝐴𝐴 𝑐𝑐 sin � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 � = 𝐴𝐴 𝑠𝑠 sin � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 33 𝐸𝐸 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 � + 𝐵𝐵 𝑠𝑠 cos � 𝜔𝜔 𝑣𝑣 33 𝐸𝐸 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 � (25)
div.p: Second, the stress should be continuous at 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ . The stress of the center part at 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ for all PE configurations is obtained by using Eq. ( 11) and ( 16):
div.formula: 𝑋𝑋 1𝑐𝑐 = 𝜔𝜔 𝑠𝑠 11 𝐸𝐸 𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 𝐴𝐴 𝑐𝑐 cos � 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙𝜔𝜔 2𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 � - 𝑑𝑑 31 𝑠𝑠 11 𝐸𝐸 𝐸𝐸 3𝑐𝑐(26)
div.p: The stress of the side part of 𝑘𝑘 33 PE OC, SC and SE can be similarly derived by utilizing mechanical wave equations. For 𝑘𝑘 33 OC, since 𝐷𝐷 1𝑠𝑠 = 0 was assumed, we obtain:
div.formula: 𝑋𝑋 1𝑠𝑠 =
div.p: Then stress continuation condition should be achieved by equating Eq. (26) to Eq. ( 27), ( 28) and (29).
Third, the stress should be zero at each edge of the bar, which is located at 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ .

The equations can simply be expressed by using Eq. ( 27), ( 28) and ( 29), but using 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ rather than 𝑥𝑥 = 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 ⁄ For OC, we have: By this moment, for each configuration, we have three equations from three mechanical boundary conditions and three unknown constants (𝐴𝐴 𝑐𝑐 , 𝐴𝐴 𝑠𝑠 and 𝐵𝐵 𝑠𝑠 ). Solving the system of equations will provide the solution to these constants. Because only the center part is measured, only 𝐴𝐴 𝑐𝑐 is required to solve the final admittance equations.
div.formula: 𝐴𝐴

div.head: D. Solving Admittance Equation
div.p: For PE, since we measure the current (admittance) through the center electrode, we have:
div.formula: 𝐼𝐼 = 𝑗𝑗𝜔𝜔 � 𝐷𝐷 3𝑐𝑐 𝑑𝑑𝑥𝑥𝑑𝑑𝑦𝑦 = 2𝑗𝑗𝜔𝜔𝑤𝑤(� 𝐷𝐷 3𝑐𝑐 𝑑𝑑𝑥𝑥) 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙 2 0 (33
div.p: Then using constitutive equations, it is obtained:
div.formula: 𝐼𝐼 = 2𝑗𝑗𝜔𝜔𝑤𝑤 � 𝐴𝐴 𝑐𝑐 𝑑𝑑 31 𝑠𝑠 11 𝐸𝐸 sin � 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙𝜔𝜔 2𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 � + 𝑎𝑎𝑙𝑙𝜀𝜀 0 𝜀𝜀 33 𝑋𝑋 �1 -𝑘𝑘 31 2 � 𝐸𝐸 3𝑐𝑐 2 � (34)
div.p: The expression for admittance is simply given by:
div.formula: 𝑌𝑌 = - 𝐼𝐼 𝑉𝑉 = 𝐼𝐼 𝐸𝐸 3𝑐𝑐 𝑡𝑡 (35)
div.p: Finally, the expression for the admittance for all the configuration is given by:
div.formula: 𝑌𝑌 𝑂𝑂𝑂𝑂 = 𝑗𝑗𝑤𝑤 � 2𝑑𝑑 31 * 2
div.p: For the final admittance equations, it is noteworthy to see the superscript star (*) on the physical parameters, meaning the parameters are complex, including real and imaginary (loss) parts. These parameters can be written as:
div.formula: 𝜀𝜀 33 𝑋𝑋 * = 𝜀𝜀 33 𝑋𝑋 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝛿𝛿 33 ′ ) (39
𝑠𝑠 11 𝐸𝐸 * = 𝑠𝑠 11 𝐸𝐸 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜙𝜙 11 ′ ) (40) 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 * = 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′′′ ) (41) 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸 * = 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′ ) (42) 𝑑𝑑 31 * = 𝑑𝑑 31 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜃𝜃 31 ′ ) (43
𝑑𝑑 33 * = 𝑑𝑑 33 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜃𝜃 33 ′ ) (44
𝑘𝑘 31 * = 𝑘𝑘 31 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜒𝜒 31 ) (45) 𝑘𝑘 33 * = 𝑘𝑘 33 (1 -𝑗𝑗 tan 𝜒𝜒 33 )(46)
div.p: In Eq. ( 41), The corresponding loss of 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 is not exactly the extensive elastic loss (tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ), but it is described as triple-prime loss (tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′′′ ) due to mechanical constraint difference with the 𝑘𝑘 𝑡𝑡 mode. It is given by [10]:
div.formula: tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′′′ = 1 𝑄𝑄 𝐵𝐵,33 = 1 1 -𝑘𝑘 33 2 [tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′ -𝑘𝑘 33 2 (2 tan 𝜃𝜃 33 ′ -tan 𝛿𝛿 33 ′ )](47)
div.p: tan 𝜙𝜙 33 can rather be directly determined from thickness mode (𝑘𝑘 𝑡𝑡 ) specimen.

div.head: E. Mathematical Limit of Analytical Solutions
div.p: So far, analytical solutions for 𝑘𝑘 33 PE are derived. One of validation methods for analytical solutions is to take limit of the admittance functions. If we take the center portion "a" as 100 % (𝑎𝑎 → 1) for all the admittance equations, meaning the center part takes up the entire PE geometry, we obtain:
div.formula: 𝑌𝑌 = 𝑗𝑗𝜔𝜔 𝜀𝜀 33 𝑋𝑋 𝑤𝑤𝑙𝑙 𝑡𝑡 �(1 -𝑘𝑘 31 2 ) + 𝑘𝑘 31 2 𝑡𝑡𝑎𝑎𝑠𝑠(𝜔𝜔𝑙𝑙 2𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 ⁄ ) 𝜔𝜔𝑙𝑙 2𝑣𝑣 11 𝐸𝐸 ⁄ �(48)
div.p: As a result of 𝑎𝑎 → 1, all the equations beautifully converge to that of Standard 𝑘𝑘 31 mode [7].

It is obvious from the fact that the center part is 𝑘𝑘 31 mode for all the PE geometry. Next, when "a" goes to zero (𝑎𝑎 → 0), antiresonance frequency (𝑓𝑓 𝐵𝐵 ) of PE OC should approach to that of standard specimen, while resonance frequency (𝑓𝑓 𝐴𝐴 ) of PE SC should approach to that of standard sample, since PE OC corresponds to antiresonance behavior and PE SC corresponds to resonance behavior for 𝑘𝑘 33 mode. Figure 3 shows analytical admittance curves for standard sample [15] and for PE OC and SC with varying "𝑎𝑎". In Figure 3, it clearly shows that the derived admittance curves for OC and SC approaches to 𝑓𝑓 𝐵𝐵 and 𝑓𝑓 𝐴𝐴 , respectively, of standard 𝑘𝑘 33 mode. It should be note that PE OC case is more sensitive to the portion of "𝑎𝑎" than PE SC case. Therefore, different from parameter determination method for 𝑘𝑘 31 PE, in which the parameters are calculated through the certain equations [5] , 𝑘𝑘 33 PE analytical solutions are required to fit into corresponding experimental data in order to determine 𝑘𝑘 33 mode-related parameters.

div.head: IV. Comparison with Finite Element Analysis (FEA)
div.p: The mathematical limit partially validates analytical solutions; they should be compared to both finite element analysis (FEA) for definite evaluation. Figure 4 shows direct comparisons of admittance curves of PE OC, SC and SE between analytical solution and FEA.

For FEA simulation, the parameters of PZT 5A, which are already built in the software, were utilized. The physical parameters and losses of PZT 5A are listed in Table 1. It should be noted that due to loss isotropy in ATILA FEA software, the subscripted indices are denoted as (i,j).

For the calculation, the piezoelectric loss was set to be 1.6 %, about the average of the sum of dielectric and elastic loss. The discrepancies between admittance curves from analytical solution and ATILA FEA is mainly due to two reasons: (1) 3D analysis in ATILA, whereas 1D analysis in the analytical solutions. Due to 3D consideration of FEA, the peak heights of admittance curves from FEA are smaller than those of admittance curves from analytical solutions. (2) FEA's consideration of higher harmonic generation, while analytical solution only considers fundamental resonance. Due to this difference, the base lines far from resonance and antiresonance frequencies of admittance curves deviate. Despite these discrepancies, only about 0.1 % deviation occurs in 𝑓𝑓 𝐴𝐴 and 𝑓𝑓 𝐵𝐵 , and about 2 % deviation occurs in 𝑄𝑄 𝐴𝐴 and 𝑄𝑄 𝐵𝐵 .
These values are especially important, since they are directly related to physical parameters and loss determination.

div.head: V. Fitting of analytical solutions to experimental data
div.p: After analytical solutions were validated with the aid of FEA, they were fitted to experimental data. Since the center part is 𝑘𝑘 31 mode, the related physical parameters were measured with standard 𝑘𝑘 31 sample. Determined parameters of 𝑘𝑘 31 mode are listed in Table 2. The parameter determination method for 𝑘𝑘 31 mode follows the one in Zhuang et al. [8].

For curve fitting, in order to simplify and minimize the fitting parameters, the following method was utilized: first, 𝜌𝜌 was determined by measuring the mass of each sample. Also, "𝑎𝑎", the center portion, was measured with the help of optical microscope. Although we designed 𝑎𝑎 as
The imaginary part of electromechanical coupling factor (tan 𝜒𝜒 33 ) are defined as "coupling loss", which is given by:
div.formula: tan 𝜒𝜒 33 = 2 tan 𝜃𝜃 33 ′ -tan 𝛿𝛿 33 ′ -tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′(51)
div.p: The determined values of physical parameters and losses from curve fitting are shown in Table 3. The fitting method can be validated by comparing 𝑘𝑘 33 value determined from two different methods, by following Eq. ( 49) and (50). 𝑘𝑘 33 determined from Eq. ( 49) is 70.3 % and that determined from Eq. ( 50) is 69.6 %; these are in good agreement.

div.head: VII. Conclusion
div.p: In summary, the "Partial Electrode" configuration for 𝑘𝑘 33 mode was introduced in order to resolve issues in the IEEE Standard 𝑘𝑘 33 mode specimen, which can determine experimentally both "intrinsic" and "extrinsic" physical parameters in piezo-ceramics. The analytical solutions for 𝑘𝑘 33 PE have been derived, with the aid of piezoelectric constitutive equations and electrical/mechanical boundary conditions. The derived admittance curves from analytical solutions well match with those simulated from ATILA FEA, in terms of resonance frequencies and mechanical quality factors. After analytical solutions are validated through the comparison, they were fitted to real experimental data to determine physical parameters of 𝑘𝑘 33 mode. The fitting method was confirmed by comparing 𝑘𝑘 33 values determined from two different equations. The fitting method to determine physical parameters and losses with analytical solutions will provide more accurate parameter determination compared to the method using the IEEE standard 𝑘𝑘 33 specimen by providing much larger capacitance with less fringing electric field effect, free of specimen setup issue, and direct determination of intensive elastic compliance and losses.

figure.head: Figure 1 .Figure 2 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 1. The schematic diagrams of (a) IEEE Standard k33 mode piezoelectric specimen, (b)

figure.head: Figure 3 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 3. (a) Frequency dependence of analytical admittance curve plot for standard and SC

figure.head: Figure 4 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 4. Comparison of admittance curves of analytical solutions and from ATILA FEA

10 % of the total length, about ± 20 % deviation occurred. Therefore, it is recommended to directly measure 𝑎𝑎 for fitting with less error. By using the center portion, 𝜀𝜀 33 𝑋𝑋 and tan 𝛿𝛿 33Finally, using already determined parameters from the previous process, 𝑑𝑑33 and tan 𝜃𝜃 33 ′ were determined by using SC sample. It should be noted that 𝑑𝑑 31 , which was determined in advance by measuring standard 𝑘𝑘 31 sample, was underestimated by about -10 % to generate good fittings. The reason may be attributed to the imperfect poling issue at the boundary between the center and side portions. However, varying 𝑑𝑑 31 in analytical solutions does not have significant effect on 𝑓𝑓 𝐴𝐴 , 𝑓𝑓 𝐵𝐵 , 𝑄𝑄 𝐴𝐴 and 𝑄𝑄 𝐵𝐵 ; rather, 𝑑𝑑 31 is just the "actuator" parameter, 𝑑𝑑 33 and tan 𝜃𝜃 33 ′ ) depend on 𝑓𝑓 𝐴𝐴 , 𝑓𝑓 𝐵𝐵 , 𝑄𝑄 𝐴𝐴 and 𝑄𝑄 𝐵𝐵 , the values of determined parameters can be considered valid. Figure 5 shows experimental data fitted to analytical solutions. Considering the underestimation of 𝑑𝑑 31 during the fitting process, analytical solution gives out excellent fitting results, with the percentage error of less than 1 %.

row.cell: determined from SE specimen, it can be obtained:
′ were measured in off-resonance regime (at 1 kHz), from capacitance and phase lag, 𝑘𝑘 33 2 = 2 𝑑𝑑 33 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸 𝜀𝜀 0 𝜀𝜀 33 𝑋𝑋 (49)
respectively. Then, by using OC specimen, 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 and tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′′′ were determined with fitting. It Also, from 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 determined from OC specimen and 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸 determined from SE specimen, it can
should be noted that the fringing "output" electric field occurring in the side of OC can be calculated:

overestimate the value of 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 , as it occurs in standard specimen. Therefore, by utilizing the finite element results in our paper [11], 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 value was calibrated by considering aspect ratio (1 -𝑘𝑘 33 𝐷𝐷 𝑠𝑠 33 2 ) = 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸
of the sample. Next, by using SE specimen, 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸 and tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′
not the key "side-load" parameter. Since all the parameters required to be determined from
fitting (𝑠𝑠 33 𝐷𝐷 , tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′′′ , 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸 , tan 𝜙𝜙 33 ′ , VI. Discussion
After all possible fitting parameters are determined, the real part of Eq. (46), which is
called electromechanical coupling factors ( 𝑘𝑘 33 ) can be calculated in two ways. With 𝜀𝜀 33 𝑋𝑋
determined from off-resonance measurement, 𝑑𝑑 33 determined from SC specimen and 𝑠𝑠 33 𝐸𝐸

figure.figDesc: Physical parameter inputs for PZT 5A that was used for ATILA FEA simulation. Due to isotropic consideration of losses in ATILA, the subscripted indices in losses are represented as (i,j).

div.head: Acknowledgement
div.p: This work was supported by Office of Naval Research under Grant Number N00014-17-1-2088

analytic.title: Heat generation in multilayer piezoelectric actuators
High power characterization of piezoelectric materials
Crystallographic approach to obtain intensive elastic parameters of k33 mode piezoelectric ceramics
Finite element modeling of functionally graded piezoelectric harvesters
Finite element characterisation of multilayer d31 piezoelectric macro-fibre composites
Methods of Measurement for Piezoelectric Vibrators
Derivation of piezoelectric losses from admittance spectra
Advanced methodology for measuring the extensive elastic compliance and mechanical loss directly in k31 mode piezoelectric ceramic plates

monogr.title: Loss Phenomenology and the Methodology to Derive Loss Factors in Piezoelectric Ceramics
Improvement of the Standard Characterization Method on k33 Mode Piezoelectric Specimens

analytic.title: Effect of depolarization field on electro-elastic properties of photoconductive CdS crystals (experimental)
Anisotropy of the high-power piezoelectric properties of Pb (Zr, Ti) O3
Loss determination methodology for a piezoelectric ceramic: new phenomenological theory and experimental proposals

biblStruct.note: with (a) OC (Purple open circle), (b) SC (Sky blue open circle) and (c) SE configuration (Green open circle). Black solid line shows analytical admittance curves fitted to experimental data
